Multiple outcomes, multiple choices: Understanding superiority in the multivariate context

Bayesian analysis
multivariate analysis
decision rules
bmco
Author

Xynthia Kavelaars

Published

June 2, 2026

On this page

  • The Challenge: How Do We Decide?
  • Four Decision Rules Visualized
    • What the Rules Mean
  • The Key Insight from Visualization
  • Setting Weights
  • Sample Size Consequences
  • Practical Example: The CAR-B Study
  • R Implementation
  • Takeaway

The Challenge: How Do We Decide?

Imagine you’re evaluating a new cancer treatment. It reduces tumor size in most patients, but some experience severe side effects. How do you decide if the treatment is “better” than the standard?

This is the fundamental challenge of clinical trials with multiple outcomes. We rarely care about just one thing. We want efficacy and safety, symptom relief and quality of life. But how do we combine these into a single decision?

The traditional approach—test each outcome separately—has problems. It ignores that outcomes are often correlated, and it forces an artificial choice: demand improvement on all outcomes (too strict) or on any outcome (too lenient).

The Kavelaars et al. (2020) framework offers a principled alternative: flexible decision rules that let us define what “success” actually means in our clinical context.

Four Decision Rules Visualized

Let me show you the core insight using a simple two-outcome example. Imagine we’re testing a treatment on: - Cognitive function (outcome 1) - Fatigue (outcome 2)

Each outcome has a treatment effect: the difference in success probability between the new treatment and control, \(\delta_1\) and \(\delta_2\).

library(tidyverse)
library(mvtnorm)

# Simulate bivariate posterior distributions of treatment differences
set.seed(2024)

# Parameters
n_samples <- 10000
mean_delta <- c(0.15, 0.12)  # positive effects on both outcomes
cov_matrix <- matrix(c(0.01, 0.005, 0.005, 0.01), nrow = 2)

# Sample from bivariate normal (posterior approximation)
delta_samples <- rmvnorm(n_samples, mean = mean_delta, sigma = cov_matrix)

# Create data frame for ggplot
df_posterior <- data.frame(
  delta1 = delta_samples[, 1],
  delta2 = delta_samples[, 2]
)

# Define superiority regions for different rules
df_plot <- df_posterior %>%
  sample_n(2000) %>%  # Plot subset for clarity
  mutate(
    Single = delta1 > 0,
    Any = delta1 > 0 | delta2 > 0,
    All = delta1 > 0 & delta2 > 0,
    Compensatory = 0.5 * delta1 + 0.5 * delta2 > 0
  )

# Visualize all four rules
rules_list <- list(
  Single = list(label = "Single Rule\n(Cognitive > 0)", color = "#E69F00"),
  Any = list(label = "Any Rule\n(At least one > 0)", color = "#56B4E9"),
  All = list(label = "All Rule\n(Both > 0)", color = "#009E73"),
  Compensatory = list(label = "Compensatory Rule\n(Weighted sum > 0)", color = "#D55E00")
)

plots <- lapply(names(rules_list), function(rule_name) {
  ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = delta1, y = delta2)) +
    geom_point(aes(color = .data[[rule_name]]), size = 1, alpha = 0.6) +
    scale_color_manual(
      values = c("FALSE" = "#CCCCCC", "TRUE" = rules_list[[rule_name]]$color),
      guide = "none"
    ) +
    geom_abline(slope = -1, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50", alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "black", alpha = 0.3) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "solid", color = "black", alpha = 0.3) +
    coord_fixed(ratio = 1) +
    labs(
      title = rules_list[[rule_name]]$label,
      x = "Treatment effect on Cognitive Function (δ₁)",
      y = "Treatment effect on Fatigue (δ₂)"
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 12, face = "bold"),
      axis.text = element_text(size = 9)
    )
})

# Combine plots
gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  plots[[1]], plots[[2]], plots[[3]], plots[[4]], 
  nrow = 2, ncol = 2
)

What the Rules Mean

1. Single Rule - Tests if cognitive function improves: \(\delta_1 > 0\) - Ignores fatigue completely - Most lenient on first outcome, ignores second - When to use: When you have one clearly primary outcome

2. Any Rule - Tests if at least one outcome improves: \(\delta_1 > 0\) OR \(\delta_2 > 0\) - Light gray regions = rejected by this rule - Most liberal: accepts many marginal improvements - When to use: Rare, unless any improvement is clinically meaningful

3. All Rule - Tests if both improve: \(\delta_1 > 0\) AND \(\delta_2 > 0\) - Darkest region in the figure - Most conservative: requires improvement everywhere - When to use: When all outcomes are truly necessary (safety + efficacy)

4. Compensatory Rule - Tests if weighted sum exceeds zero: \(0.5 \cdot \delta_1 + 0.5 \cdot \delta_2 > 0\) - The diagonal dashed line shows the decision boundary - Allows tradeoffs: A strong gain in one outcome can compensate for a small loss in another - When to use: Most clinical scenarios (flexibility + clinical sense)

The Key Insight from Visualization

Notice the dark blue points (correct decisions) in each plot. The diagonal orientation of the superiority region in the Compensatory rule is crucial:

The posterior distribution is tilted relative to the axes. If both outcomes are correlated positively, a diagonal boundary efficiently captures this relationship. This is why multivariate analysis is more efficient than univariate: it respects the actual geometry of your data.

Setting Weights

The Compensatory rule gets even more flexible:

# Equal weights: both outcomes equally important
weights_equal <- c(0.50, 0.50)

# Unequal weights: cognitive function more important
weights_unequal <- c(0.75, 0.25)

# Decision boundary: w1*delta1 + w2*delta2 > 0
# With unequal weights, the slope changes:
# y = -(w1/w2) * x = -(0.75/0.25) * x = -3x

# This steeper line means:
# - Harder to compensate a negative effect on cognitive (weight 0.75)
# - Easier to accept a negative effect on fatigue (weight 0.25)

Sample Size Consequences

Here’s why this matters practically. In the paper’s simulation:

Rule Sample size (equal effects) Notes
Single 75–307 Varies wildly by which outcome
Any 47–247 Liberal, smallest n
All 101–424 Conservative, largest n
Compensatory (equal) 26–199 Most efficient when both effects present

The Compensatory rule wins because it leverages the correlation structure your data actually exhibits.

Practical Example: The CAR-B Study

From the paper: Should local brain radiation (SRS) replace whole-brain radiation (WBRT) for cancer patients?

Two outcomes matter: - Cognitive function (higher = better) - Fatigue (lower = better, but we flip it to “improvement” = less fatigue)

Traditional analysis might demand improvement on both (All rule). But what if SRS slightly hurts cognition while dramatically reducing fatigue? With equal-weight Compensatory, you’d properly recognize this as overall superior.

R Implementation

The bmco package implements this exactly:

library(bmco)

result <- bmvb(
  data = trial_data,
  grp = "treatment",
  grp_a = "control",
  grp_b = "new_treatment",
  y_vars = c("cognitive_function", "fatigue_reduction"),
  rule = "Comp",           # Use Compensatory
  w = c(0.60, 0.40),       # Cognitive 60%, fatigue 40%
  n_it = 5000
)

result$delta$pop  # P(treatment superior under our rule)

Takeaway

The decision rule you choose shapes your entire trial:

  • Single: Use only for truly secondary outcomes
  • Any: Too liberal for most clinical contexts
  • All: Only if all outcomes are absolutely necessary
  • Compensatory: Default choice; lets you encode clinical judgment about tradeoffs

The beauty of the multivariate Bernoulli framework is that you don’t have to pick between statistical power and clinical relevance. You can have both—by making your decision rule match your actual clinical question.

Reference: Kavelaars, X., Mulder, J. & Kaptein, M. (2020). Decision-making with multiple correlated binary outcomes in clinical trials. Statistical Methods in Medical Research. https://doi.org/10.1177/0962280220922256

Package: Install with install.packages("bmco")

Questions? Found this useful? I’m on social media and happy to discuss!
